content: So he finished, turning to the fat little man, with the pipe, sitting on the sofa.
The latter removed his pipe from his lips with dignity and observed severely:
What do you mean?" answered Kalganov suddenly.
"Sit down with us.
How are you?"
"Delighted to see you, dear ... and precious fellow, I always thought a lot of you." Mitya responded, joyfully and eagerly, at once holding out his hand across the table.
How tight you squeeze!
She was watching him with intense curiosity and still some uneasiness.
She was impressed by something about him, and indeed the last thing she expected of him was that he would come in and speak like this at such a moment.
"Good evening," Maximov ventured blandly on the left.
"Good evening.
You're here, too!
How glad I am to find you here, too! Gentlemen, gentlemen, I-" (He addressed the Polish gentleman with the pipe again, evidently taking him for the most important person present.) "I flew here....
I wanted to spend my last day, my last hour in this room, in this very room ... where I, too, adored ... my queen....
Let's drink to our good understanding.
They'll bring the wine at once....
I want to have music, singing, a revel, as we had before.
I will commemorate my day of joy on my last night."
He was almost choking.
'Sovereign,' I suppose?" interrupted Grushenka. "I can't help laughing at you, the way you talk.
Sit down, Mitya, what are you talking about?
Don't frighten us, please.
You won't frighten us, will you?
If you won't, I am glad to see you ..."
"Oh, pass me by, go your way, I won't hinder you!..."
"Come, come, what a fellow you are!" cried Grushenka reproachfully. "That's just how he comes to see me-he begins talking, and I can't make out what he means.
He cried like that once before, and now he's crying again!
It's shameful!
Why are you crying?
"Well, there you are again....
Come, cheer up, cheer up!" Grushenka said to him persuasively.
"I'm very glad you've come, very glad, Mitya, do you hear, I'm very glad!
"I wish it, I wish it! And if he goes away I shall go, too!" she added with flashing eyes.
"What my queen commands is law!" pronounced the Pole, gallantly kissing Grushenka's hand.
Every one laughed.
"Good heavens!
I want some myself, and I can't bear liqueurs.
We were fearfully dull here....
You've come for a spree again, I suppose?
But put your money in your pocket.
Where did you get such a lot?"
He flushed.
At that moment the innkeeper brought in an uncorked bottle of champagne, and glasses on a tray.
Mitya snatched up the bottle, but he was so bewildered that he did not know what to do with it.
Kalganov took it from him and poured out the champagne.
"Another!
His whole countenance suddenly changed.
The solemn and tragic expression with which he had entered vanished completely, and a look of something childlike came into his face.
He seemed to have become suddenly gentle and subdued.
He looked shyly and happily at every one, with a continual nervous little laugh, and the blissful expression of a dog who has done wrong, been punished, and forgiven.
He seemed to have forgotten everything, and was looking round at every one with a childlike smile of delight.
He looked at Grushenka, laughing continually, and bringing his chair close up to her.
"Well, what of it?
It's a good thing he's smoking a pipe," he reflected.
The Pole's puffy, middle aged face, with its tiny nose and two very thin, pointed, dyed and impudent looking mustaches, had not so far roused the faintest doubts in Mitya.
He was not even particularly struck by the Pole's absurd wig made in Siberia, with love locks foolishly combed forward over the temples.
It occurred to him, too, that this Pole must be the friend of the other, as it were, a "bodyguard," and no doubt the big Pole was at the disposal of the little Pole with the pipe.
But this all seemed to Mitya perfectly right and not to be questioned.
In his mood of doglike submissiveness all feeling of rivalry had died away.
Grushenka's mood and the enigmatic tone of some of her words he completely failed to grasp.
All he understood, with thrilling heart, was that she was kind to him, that she had forgiven him, and made him sit by her.
He was beside himself with delight, watching her sip her glass of champagne.
"Why are we sitting here though, gentlemen?
Why don't you begin doing something?" his smiling eyes seemed to ask.
"He's talking nonsense?" he laughed, his short, wooden laugh, seeming suddenly delighted at something-"ha ha!"
"Yes.
Would you believe it, he will have it that all our cavalry officers in the twenties married Polish women.
That's awful rot, isn't it?"
"Polish women?" repeated Mitya, perfectly ecstatic.
Grushenka he knew before, and had once been with some one to see her; but she had not taken to him.
As a rule he was very willful, even capricious, though always friendly.
He would look at you and listen, seeming all the while to be persistently dreaming over something else.
"Only imagine, I've been taking him about with me for the last four days," he went on, indolently drawling his words, quite naturally though, without the slightest affectation.
"Ever since your brother, do you remember, shoved him off the carriage and sent him flying.
I'm taking him back."
"The gentleman has not seen Polish ladies, and says what is impossible," the Pole with the pipe observed to Maximov.
He spoke Russian fairly well, much better, anyway, than he pretended.
"Was he a cavalry officer indeed?
Ha ha!" cried Mitya, listening eagerly, and turning his inquiring eyes to each as he spoke, as though there were no knowing what he might hear from each.
"No, you see," Maximov turned to him.
The dress of both the Poles looked rather greasy.
What's he scolding about?" said Grushenka, suddenly vexed.
"You can reckon on that," the tall Pole snapped contemptuously.
"What next!
Let him talk!
People talk, why hinder them?
It makes it cheerful," Grushenka said crossly.
"No, no
"He's never been in Poland, so how can he talk about it?
I suppose you weren't married in Poland, were you?"
He brought her straight from Poland and gave her up to me.
But he didn't marry her, because she turned out to be lame."
"So you married a lame woman?" cried Kalganov.
"Yes.
They both deceived me a little bit at the time, and concealed it.
I thought she was hopping; she kept hopping....
I thought it was for fun."
"So pleased she was going to marry you!" yelled Kalganov, in a ringing, childish voice.
"Yes, so pleased.
But it turned out to be quite a different cause. Afterwards, when we were married, after the wedding, that very evening, she confessed, and very touchingly asked forgiveness.
Kalganov went off into the most childish laughter, almost falling on the sofa.
Grushenka, too, laughed.
But his second wife, do you know, ran away, and is alive now."
"Is it possible?" said Mitya, turning quickly to Maximov with an expression of the utmost astonishment.
"Yes.
She did run away.
And what was worse, she'd had all my little property transferred to her beforehand.
'You're an educated man,' she said to me.
'You can always get your living.' She settled my business with that.
"Listen, listen!" cried Kalganov, bubbling over, "if he's telling lies-and he often is-he's only doing it to amuse us all.
There's no harm in that, is there?
You know, I sometimes like him.
Don't you think so?
Some people are low from self interest, but he's simply so, from nature.
Do you remember, there's a landowner called Maximov in it, whom Nozdryov thrashed.
He couldn't have been thrashed then, he couldn't, could he?"
"Well, but if they did thrash him!" he cried, laughing.
"What do you mean?
Either they thrashed you or they didn't."
The other shrugged his shoulders in reply.
Neither of them had a watch.
"Why not talk?
Let other people talk.
Mustn't other people talk because you're bored?" Grushenka flew at him with evident intention of finding fault.
I didn't say anything."
"All right then.
Come, tell us your story," Grushenka cried to Maximov. "Why are you all silent?"
"There's nothing to tell, it's all so foolish," answered Maximov at once, with evident satisfaction, mincing a little.
"Besides, all that's by way of allegory in Gogol, for he's made all the names have a meaning.
"But what were you beaten for?" cried Kalganov.
"For Piron!" answered Maximov.
We were all drinking then, a big party of us, in a tavern at that very fair.
They'd invited me, and first of all I began quoting epigrams.
They seized me and thrashed me."
"But what for?
That's all stupid, I don't want to listen.
Let us drink."
Drink, most illustrious, take your glass!" Mitya urged.
"Pan Vrublevsky," put in the Pole on the sofa.
"Pour out some for us," said Grushenka; "I'll drink to Russia, too!"
"And I would, too ... to Russia, the old grandmother!" tittered Maximov.
"All!
"Trifon Borissovitch, some more bottles!"
All drank the toast except the Poles, and Grushenka tossed off her whole glass at once.
The Poles did not touch theirs.
"To Russia as she was before seventeen seventy two."
"Come, that's better!" cried the other Pole, and they both emptied their glasses at once.
"Can one help loving one's own country?" he shouted.
"Be silent!
Don't quarrel!
I won't have any quarreling!" cried Grushenka imperiously, and she stamped her foot on the floor.
Her face glowed, her eyes were shining.
"Hold your tongue, you, anyway!
Sit down, you stupid!" Grushenka scolded with angry annoyance.
"Come, why are we sitting here?
What shall we do ... to amuse ourselves again?"
"Let's play faro again, as we did just now," Maximov tittered suddenly.
"Faro?
"That's true," assented Pan Vrublevsky.
"Lite?
"It's always late with them.
They can never do anything!" Grushenka almost shrieked in her anger.
"They're dull themselves, so they want others to be dull.
"I want to lose a lot to you.
Take your cards.
Make the bank."
"From the landlord?
And he did not know himself what orders to give and why he had run out.
He only told them to take out of the box the presents for the girls, the sweets, the toffee and the fondants.
"And vodka for Andrey, vodka for Andrey!" he cried in haste.
"I was rude to Andrey!"
Suddenly Maximov, who had followed him out, touched him on the shoulder.
"Capital!
Splendid!
Take ten, here!"
Again he took all the notes out of his pocket and picked out one for ten roubles.
"And if you lose that, come again, come again."
The Poles had already sat down, and opened the pack.
They looked much more amiable, almost cordial.
"To your places, gentlemen," cried Pan Vrublevsky.
"No, I'm not going to play any more," observed Kalganov, "I've lost fifty roubles to them just now."
"What Podvysotsky?"
Podvysotsky wins.
"That's not true," said Kalganov.
You see how I talk Polish, ha ha!
Here, I stake ten roubles, the knave leads."
The rouble won, too.
"A corner!" cried Mitya.
"I'll bet another rouble, a 'single' stake," Maximov muttered gleefully, hugely delighted at having won a rouble.
"A 'double' on the seven!"
The seven too was trumped.
"Stop!" cried Kalganov suddenly.
"Double!
The rouble stakes kept winning.
Will you stake another hundred?" the Pole on the sofa inquired.
"What?
Lost two hundred already?
Then another two hundred!
All doubles!"
And pulling his money out of his pocket, Mitya was about to fling two hundred roubles on the queen, but Kalganov covered it with his hand.
"That's enough!" he shouted in his ringing voice.
"That's enough!
I don't want you to play any more.
Don't!"
"Why?"
"Because I don't.
Hang it, come away.
That's why.
He may be right.
Both the Poles rose from their seats with a deeply offended air.
"Don't dare to shout like that," cried Grushenka.
But something in Grushenka's face suddenly struck him, and at the same instant something new flashed into his mind-a strange new thought!
"What do you want?"
"In the next room, I've two words to say to you, something pleasant, very pleasant.
You'll be glad to hear it."
"The bodyguard?
Let him come, and I want him, too.
I must have him!" cried Mitya.
There was a sort of boldness, a sudden confidence shining in his eyes.
His face had looked very different when he entered the room an hour before.
There was a lighted candle on a small deal table in the corner.
There's money for you," he pulled out his notes.
"Would you like three thousand?
Take it and go your way."
The Pole gazed open eyed at Mitya, with a searching look.
But, at once, this very minute, and for ever.
Here's the door, you go out of it.
What have you got there, a great coat, a fur coat?
I'll bring it out to you.
He had no doubts.
An expression of extraordinary resolution passed over the Pole's face.
The Poles exchanged glances again.
The short man's face looked more forbidding.
"Seven hundred, seven hundred, not five hundred, at once, this minute, cash down!" Mitya added, feeling something wrong.
Don't you trust me?
I can't give you the whole three thousand straight off.
"Upon my word, the money's there, hidden."
"What next?" he asked ironically.
You're a couple of capons, that's what you are!"
"This is a mortal insult!"
And so indeed he did.
The Pole walked into the room and threw himself in a theatrical attitude before Grushenka.
"Speak Russian!
Speak Russian!" she cried, "not another word of Polish! You used to talk Russian.
She was red with passion.
"My name's Agrafena, Grushenka, speak Russian or I won't listen!"
"Forgive?
Came here to forgive me?" Grushenka cut him short, jumping up from her seat.
"What?
He offered you money for me?" cried Grushenka, hysterically.
How dare you?
"He took it!
He took it!" cried Mitya; "only he wanted to get the whole three thousand at once, and I could only give him seven hundred straight off."
"I see: he heard I had money, and came here to marry me!"
I came here to make you my wife and I find you a different woman, perverse and shameless."
"Oh, go back where you came from!
"I've been a fool, a fool, to have been miserable these five years!
And it wasn't for his sake, it was my anger made me miserable.
And this isn't he at all!
Was he like this?
It might be his father!
Where did you get your wig from?
He was a falcon, but this is a gander.
He used to laugh and sing to me....
And I've been crying for five years, damned fool, abject, shameless I was!"
She sank back in her low chair and hid her face in her hands.
"Landlord, send the shameless hussies away!"
"What are you shouting for?
"Animal?
And what sort of cards were you playing with just now?
I gave you a pack and you hid it.
You played with marked cards!
"Here's my pack unopened!"
"How shameful!
"Good Lord, he's come to that!"
"You low harlot!"
"I've laid him on the floor, there," he announced, returning at once, gasping with excitement.
"He's struggling, the scoundrel!
It's as good as stolen from you."
"I don't want my fifty roubles back," Kalganov declared suddenly.
Let him keep it as a consolation."
You're a trump, Mitya!" cried Grushenka, and there was a note of fierce anger in the exclamation.
If not, good by."
And swelling with indignation and importance he went to the door.
